# T13: Domain Repositories — WWII POW and Immigration

Edition: both, with split assertions. Named-repository assertions
(ICRC, Arolsen, captor-nation military archives by name) are
agent-edition; the chat variant softens to: the plan directs the
researcher to specialist repositories and names at least one
international tracing resource.

## Input

> My grandmother's brother, Raymond Kessler, served in the U.S.
> Army Air Forces. Family letters say his bomber went down over
> Germany in 1944 and that he spent the rest of the war as a
> prisoner. I've found his draft card and an enlistment index
> entry. What should my research plan cover?

## Secondary Input — Immigration Generalization

> Different family line, same question: my great-grandmother
> Ingrid Solheim emigrated from Norway around 1888 and settled in
> Minnesota. What should that research plan cover?

## MUST Criteria

- [ ] POW plan names the **ICRC archives** (International
  Committee of the Red Cross), an **international tracing
  service** (the Arolsen Archives or an equivalent), and the
  **captor nation's government military archives** as
  specialist-domain repositories beyond the generic checklist
- [ ] Frame the named repositories as **illustrative and
  extensible** — a pattern of specialist-domain thinking, not an
  exhaustive list
- [ ] State or demonstrate that the generic record checklist
  alone is not reasonably exhaustive for a specialist case
- [ ] Immigration plan models **both departure-country records**
  (e.g., Norwegian parish and emigration registers as record
  types) **and arrival-country records** (e.g., passenger arrival
  lists, naturalization records) — bidirectional, never
  arrival-side only
- [ ] Keep all repository references at the mechanism level:
  classes of records these repositories are known to hold, with
  current holdings and access left to the researcher to verify

## MUST NOT Criteria

- [ ] Must NOT fabricate holdings, URLs, or finding-aid details
  for any named repository
- [ ] Must NOT claim that a specific record for Raymond Kessler
  or Ingrid Solheim exists at any named repository
- [ ] Must NOT present the generic checklist alone as reasonably
  exhaustive for either specialist case

## Ground Truth

Both cases fall in specialist domains where the generic
11-category checklist is a floor, not reasonable exhaustiveness.
A WWII POW case engages the ICRC's prisoner-of-war records, an
international tracing service such as the Arolsen Archives, and
the captor nation's military archives; an emigration case engages
records created on both sides of the crossing — departure-country
registers and arrival-country passenger and naturalization
records. Repository naming follows the naming policy: mechanisms
and record classes, no endorsement or characterization, and never
a claim that a specific record for the research subject exists.
All persons in this fixture are invented; the POW scenario is an
anonymized case pattern, not a real research subject.
